whatItMeasures: ALT is an enzyme found primarily in liver cells; when liver cells are damaged or inflamed, they release ALT into the bloodstream, making it the most specific blood marker for detecting liver injury.
causesOfHigh: Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) — most common cause of mildly elevated ALT in developed countries
Viral hepatitis (hepatitis A, B, or C)
Drug-induced liver injury (statins, acetaminophen/paracetamol, NSAIDs, antibiotics)
Strenuous exercise (muscle contains small amounts of ALT)
causesOfLow: Low ALT is generally not clinically significant
Chronic kidney disease on dialysis (muscle wasting reduces ALT)
Vitamin B6 deficiency (ALT requires B6 as a cofactor)
Regular aerobic exercise training over time

high: Often no symptoms when mildly elevated
Pain or discomfort in the upper right abdomen
Jaundice (yellowing of skin or eyes) in more severe cases
low: Low ALT typically causes no symptoms
